past_medical_history: HSV-1 encephalitis 6 weeks ago — confirmed by CSF PCR, treated with 21 days of IV acyclovir at General Hospital, discharged improved with mild residual word-finding difficulty
Seizure during initial HSV encephalitis hospitalization — started on levetiracetam
No other significant medical history prior to HSV encephalitis

medications.indication: Seizure prophylaxis — started during HSV encephalitis hospitalization
Maintenance antiviral therapy following HSV encephalitis

family_history: Mother: rheumatoid arthritis, managed with methotrexate
No family history of epilepsy, psychotic disorders, or other neurological disease

social_history.occupation: High school chemistry teacher — returned to light duties 2 weeks ago after recovering from HSV encephalitis
social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinking, has abstained since hospitalization 6 weeks ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in apartment. Wife is primary caregiver since HSV encephalitis.
social_history.recent_stressors: Recovering from HSV encephalitis; adjusting to return to work; concerned about cognitive recovery

neurological_exam.mental_status: GCS 13 (E3V4M6). Confused, disoriented to date and place (thinks he is at a different hospital). Affect is flat with intermittent unprovoked agitation. Speech is fluent but with frequent paraphasic errors and tangential responses. Unable to perform serial sevens. Registration 3/3, recall 0/3 at five minutes. His wife reports this is a dramatic change from 5 days ago, when he was oriented and functional at work.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal 3 mm, reactive to light bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. No papilledema. Visual fields full to confrontation. Prominent orofacial dyskinesias — intermittent lip smacking, chewing movements, and tongue protrusion every 15-30 seconds. These are NEW movements not present during the prior HSV hospitalization. Facial sensation intact. Facial strength symmetric. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Bilateral choreiform movements of the upper extremities — involuntary, irregular, flowing movements affecting hands and forearms bilaterally. These are also NEW. Intermittent catatonic posturing — arms held in fixed elevated position for 10-20 seconds when stimulated. Strength is grossly 5/5 in all extremities when patient cooperates. Tone mildly increased in bilateral upper extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Inconsistent responses — patient unable to cooperate reliably with sensory testing.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ symmetric bilaterally. Plantar responses extensor bilaterally (Babinski positive bilaterally).
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to reliably assess — choreiform movements and inattention preclude formal testing.
neurological_exam.gait: Unsteady, wide-based. Requires assistance to ambulate. Unable to perform tandem gait.
neurological_exam.additional: Two brief staring episodes observed during the 30-minute examination — each lasting approximately 20-30 seconds with behavioral arrest, orofacial automatisms (lip smacking), and subtle left arm posturing. Patient unaware of these episodes afterward. Autonomic instability present: blood pressure fluctuating between 120/74 and 148/92, heart rate ranging from 78 to 118 bpm. Temperature 37.8°C. Wife confirms these movements (chorea, lip smacking) and staring episodes are entirely new — they were NOT present during the prior HSV encephalitis hospitalization or during recovery.

patient.chief_complaint: Return of confusion and new involuntary movements after recovering from brain infection
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Carlos Reyes is a 28-year-old right-handed Hispanic man who presents to the emergency department with recurrent confusion, new involuntary movements, and a witnessed seizure, occurring 6 weeks after treatment for confirmed HSV-1 encephalitis. Six weeks ago, he was hospitalized at General Hospital for 18 days with HSV-1 encephalitis — presenting with acute confusion, fever, and left temporal lobe involvement on MRI. CSF HSV-1 PCR was positive. He was treated with 21 days of IV acyclovir and had a good recovery, with mild residual word-finding difficulty that was improving at outpatient follow-up. He was started on levetiracetam for seizure prophylaxis (had one seizure during the acute illness) and valacyclovir for maintenance antiviral therapy. He returned to light teaching duties 2 weeks ago and was doing well per his wife — oriented, independent in daily activities, and cognitively much improved. Over the past 10 days, his wife has noticed progressive personality changes. He became apathetic, stopped initiating conversation, and lost interest in activities. Then 5 days ago, he became irritable, suspicious, and confused — asking repetitive questions and getting lost in their apartment. Three days ago, she noticed new involuntary movements — his hands and arms would make flowing, dance-like movements (chorea), and he began making repetitive lip-smacking and chewing movements. Yesterday, she witnessed a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 90 seconds. She called 911 and he was brought to the ED. His wife is extremely concerned and states 'he was getting better and now it is worse than before.' The initial treating team's working hypothesis is HSV encephalitis relapse and they plan to restart IV acyclovir.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: No sustained posterior dominant rhythm identifiable
background.asymmetry: Mild left temporal slowing (consistent with prior HSV damage)

findings.frequency: 1-3 Hz delta waves with superimposed 20-30 Hz beta activity
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta activity with overlying fast (beta) bursts — characteristic extreme delta brush pattern, persistent and well-formed
findings.state: Present in wakefulness and sleep, continuous

findings.location: Right temporal onset with bilateral spread
findings.frequency: Rhythmic 4-5 Hz theta evolving over 55 seconds
findings.state: Correlating with behavioral arrest and lip-smacking automatisms

artifacts.description: Frequent choreiform movement artifact affecting multiple channels

eeg.impression: Three electrographic seizures from bilateral temporal regions captured during this 2-hour recording. Background is moderately to severely disorganized. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Frequent choreiform movement artifact partially obscuring some segments.

findings.type: Left medial temporal encephalomalacia (OLD — from prior HSV)
findings.location: Left medial temporal lobe — hippocampus and amygdala

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, well-defined borders consistent with chronic change
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion (old damage, not acute)

findings.mass_effect: Mild left temporal horn ex-vacuo dilation

findings.type: NEW bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity
findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes — RIGHT hippocampus (entirely new) and LEFT extending BEYOND the prior HSV scar territory into the left entorhinal cortex and parahippocampal gyrus

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, right > left (but left is also new beyond the old scar)
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion (argues against HSV relapse, which typically shows DWI restriction)

findings.borders: Ill-defined — consistent with active inflammatory/autoimmune process

additional_observations: No new hemorrhagic changes (HSV relapse often shows hemorrhage)
mri.impression: Left medial temporal encephalomalacia from prior HSV encephalitis — chronic, expected. NEW bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity (right greater than left) EXTENDING BEYOND the prior HSV territory. The right-sided changes are entirely new and the left-sided changes extend beyond the prior HSV scar. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 104 bpm, likely from autonomic instability and agitation. No conduction abnormalities. Normal intervals.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 133 mEq/L (L), Chloride 97 mEq/L (L), ESR 22 mm/hr (H), CK 290 U/L (H).

special_tests.Oligoclonal_bands: 6 bands present in CSF, not present in matched serum (CSF-specific — increased from 0 bands during prior HSV admission)
special_tests.Anti_NMDA_receptor_antibodies: Sent — results pending (send-out test, estimated 7-10 days)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 16 cmH2O. WBC: 32 cells/uL (elevated) (3% PMN/88% lymph). Protein: 48 mg/dL. Glucose: 62 mg/dL (ratio 0.63 (serum glucose 98 mg/dL)). HSV_1_PCR: NEGATIVE. HSV_2_PCR: Negative. VZV_PCR: Negative. Enterovirus_PCR: Negative. Gram_stain: No organisms seen. Bacterial_culture: No growth at 48 hours. Oligoclonal_bands: 6 bands present in CSF, not present in matched serum (CSF-specific — increased from 0 bands during prior HSV admission). IgG_index: 0.82 (elevated; normal <0.70).

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:256)  (H), Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:64)  (H).

additional_observations: No testicular masses on scrotal ultrasound component
output.impression: Normal CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and scrotal ultrasound. No teratoma or other tumor identified. No tumor is expected in post-HSV cases. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.type: Comparison analysis — prior admission vs current

signal_characteristics.Prior_MRI_6_weeks_ago: LEFT medial temporal restricted diffusion and FLAIR hyperintensity with mild hemorrhagic component — consistent with acute HSV encephalitis, LEFT side only. RIGHT medial temporal lobe was NORMAL.
signal_characteristics.Current_MRI: LEFT medial temporal encephalomalacia (chronic — healed HSV scar). NEW RIGHT medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity (entirely new). NEW left-sided FLAIR changes EXTENDING BEYOND the prior HSV scar into the left entorhinal cortex and parahippocampal gyrus. NO restricted diffusion (not HSV). NO hemorrhage (not HSV).

findings.mass_effect: New mild right hippocampal swelling; old left temporal volume loss

additional_observations: Prior MRI showed unilateral LEFT disease (HSV)
Current MRI shows BILATERAL disease extending beyond the original HSV territory
output.impression: The right temporal changes are entirely new, and the left temporal changes extend beyond the prior HSV scar. Clinical correlation recommended.

background.asymmetry: Mild left temporal slowing (prior HSV scar)

findings.frequency: 1-3 Hz delta with superimposed 20-30 Hz beta
findings.morphology: Well-formed and persistent extreme delta brush throughout the 24-hour recording

findings.location: Bilateral temporal — 5 right temporal onset, 3 left temporal onset
findings.state: Distributed throughout the recording; correlating with lip-smacking automatisms and staring episodes observed by nursing

artifacts.description: Choreiform movement artifact intermittently obscuring channels

output.impression: Eight electrographic seizures from bilateral temporal regions were captured, with the majority arising from the right temporal lobe (contralateral to the prior HSV damage), confirming that the seizures are from a new process. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Post-herpes simplex virus anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

differential.key_distinguishing: HSV PCR is NEGATIVE on repeat LP — the single most important finding. New bilateral temporal changes extend beyond the original unilateral HSV territory. Movement disorder (chorea, orofacial dyskinesias) and catatonia are atypical for HSV relapse. Extreme delta brush on EEG is never seen in HSV.

Extreme delta brush and orofacial dyskinesias are highly specific for NMDAR encephalitis. The temporal relationship to prior HSV encephalitis strongly suggests post-infectious NMDAR encephalitis specifically.

differential.diagnosis: Status epilepticus from prior HSV damage
differential.key_distinguishing: While HSV-damaged temporal lobe can be epileptogenic, the NEW movement disorder (chorea, orofacial dyskinesias) and catatonia are not explained by epilepsy alone. Additionally, seizures are arising from the contralateral (right) side, not just the damaged left.
No tumor found on CT body and testicular ultrasound. The clear temporal relationship to HSV encephalitis (6-week interval) strongly suggests post-infectious rather than paraneoplastic mechanism.

optimal_actions.action: Perform urgent lumbar puncture with HSV PCR and NMDAR antibodies — the HSV PCR result is the pivotal test distinguishing HSV relapse from post-HSV autoimmune encephalitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: HSV PCR NEGATIVE; lymphocytic pleocytosis; new oligoclonal bands; NMDAR antibodies pending

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI and compare with prior admission MRI to characterize new versus old changes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: New bilateral temporal changes extending beyond prior unilateral HSV territory

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG — recognize extreme delta brush as pathognomonic for NMDAR encephalitis and inconsistent with HSV relapse
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Extreme delta brush pattern; bilateral temporal seizures

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis is a known entity requiring immunotherapy, NOT antivirals
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Correct diagnostic framework shift from infectious relapse to autoimmune

optimal_actions.action: Start first-line immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x 5 days + IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) once negative HSV PCR and clinical picture support autoimmune diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Immunotherapy to halt antibody-mediated neuronal damage

optimal_actions.action: Continue levetiracetam and consider dose increase or add-on antiepileptic given high seizure burden
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Seizure management — frequent seizures common in NMDAR encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Consider second-line therapy (rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x 4 doses) if poor response to first-line immunotherapy within 2-4 weeks
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis has a higher relapse rate and may require more aggressive immunotherapy

optimal_actions.action: Tumor screening (CT body, testicular ultrasound) to exclude paraneoplastic etiology even though post-infectious is most likely
Continuous EEG monitoring for seizure burden quantification and treatment response monitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: 8 seizures per 24 hours; response to immunotherapy should be tracked

critical_actions: Repeat LP with HSV PCR — negative PCR distinguishes from HSV relapse
Recognize that post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis is a known entity requiring immunotherapy, not antivirals
Start first-line immunotherapy (steroids + IVIG or PLEX)
Consider second-line therapy (rituximab) if poor response to first-line
contraindicated_actions: Restarting/escalating acyclovir without checking HSV PCR first
Treating as HSV relapse indefinitely when PCR is negative
Attributing new symptoms to 'post-HSV sequelae' without autoimmune workup
Stopping immunotherapy prematurely (high relapse rate in post-HSV NMDAR)
key_reasoning_points: Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis occurs in 20-30% of HSV encephalitis survivors, typically 4-8 weeks after initial infection
Negative HSV PCR on repeat LP is the critical finding distinguishing post-infectious autoimmune from HSV relapse
NEW bilateral temporal changes when original HSV was unilateral suggest a different process (autoimmune) affecting both sides
Extreme delta brush on EEG is nearly pathognomonic for NMDAR encephalitis and is NOT a feature of HSV
Chorea and orofacial dyskinesias are characteristic of NMDAR encephalitis but not typical of HSV encephalitis or its relapse
In post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis, the mechanism is that HSV-damaged neurons release NMDAR antigens, triggering an autoimmune response
No tumor is expected in post-HSV cases — this is an immune-mediated, not paraneoplastic, mechanism

red_herrings.intended_effect: Strongly biases toward HSV relapse as the explanation for recurrent symptoms
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis is the most common cause of clinical deterioration after HSV encephalitis — always check for it

red_herrings.intended_effect: Prior HSV damage may be blamed for all current symptoms
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: NEW bilateral changes extending beyond the old HSV scar indicate a new process, not progression of old damage

red_herrings.data_point: Levetiracetam and valacyclovir on medication list
red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests patient is already being appropriately treated
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Valacyclovir treats HSV but not NMDAR encephalitis; the new presentation requires immunotherapy

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis presenting 6 weeks after treated HSV encephalitis. The initial treating team assumes HSV relapse, but negative HSV PCR, new bilateral MRI changes beyond the original HSV territory, extreme delta brush on EEG, and characteristic movement disorder all point to autoimmune encephalitis requiring immunotherapy rather than antivirals.
clinical_pearls: Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis occurs in 20-30% of HSV encephalitis survivors — it should be actively looked for
Negative HSV PCR on repeat LP is the single most important test to distinguish post-HSV autoimmune from HSV relapse
Extreme delta brush on EEG is pathognomonic for NMDAR encephalitis and is never seen in HSV
New bilateral temporal MRI changes when original HSV was unilateral strongly suggest autoimmune encephalitis
Chorea and orofacial dyskinesias are hallmarks of NMDAR encephalitis and do not occur in HSV encephalitis
Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis has a higher relapse rate and may require more aggressive immunotherapy (rituximab)
